Dr. Bikman, many years ago I was diagnosed with T2 Diabetes, and decided to undergo gastric bypass.  I wish so much I had never had it done. But many years ago, I had never heard of low carb, or Carnivore.  I lost all of the excess weight that I was carrying, and also T2 Diabetes.  I was able to keep that weight off (over 100 lbs) for many years - up until about 8 yrs ago, and then I quickly started gaining weight, and also Type 2 D came back.  About 5 yrs ago, I found low carb, and then 4 yrs ago - went carnivore.  I lost all of my weight - but it's still a struggle and I worry constantly about proper food digestion, getting enough nutrition,  because I am not able to eat enough.  All these years later, I find that I still cannot eat even the daily TDEE.  If I eat over 1000 calories, I gain weight.  That surgery permanently lowered my metabolism forever.  Which is really sad.  I WISH I could warn anyone and everyone who is thinking of getting weight loss surgery.  It's NOT a smart thing to do - it forever changes you in ways that are not good.

A number of years ago the military decided that waist size was the criteria for PT and if you were not under a certain waist size, you were discharged with no retirement.  It did NOT matter is you were 5'-2" or 6'-4", you must meet the waist size.  (They only did this for a little before giving people extra chances to lose weight) but my husband was part of the test group and as a tall guy, he was 2 inches over and needed to meet it in the next 4 months.  At the time Lipo seemed the only option and worth it if he got to stay in and keep his retirement.  It was so crazy, but it did work.  Fast forward many years later, post retirement, we found carnivore and he has lost back down to the size he was entering the military, so much healthier!  Lipo never helped his health, just allowed him to stay in the military...funny, he never gained the weight right where they tape, so even if he gained other places, he could still pass the tape measurement.  What a joke to lean on one tape measure as being physically fit...although he wasn't healthy, he never had to worry about being "physically fit."

Thank you for not focusing on gout.  I've had gout.  Although painful I doubt many people have died from it.  In reality, gout is a blessing in that it should alert you to hyperuricemia and the possibility of uric acid crystals elsewhere.  We may become aware of gout with a joint hurting but uric acid crystals have been found many places-- in coronary arteries, aorta, heart valves, myocardium, kidneys, prostate, and pancreas amongst others.  Crystals have even been found in atherosclerotic plaques.  Its frequency is VERY underappreciated because it's hard to detect.  Routine tissue fixation with formalin washes out uric acid crystals.  Likewise, tissue is usually cut 7 microns thick for microscopy.  Uric acid crystals are usually linear and, if they're perpendicular to the slide, will appear as a small dot.  To see uric acid crystals one has to preserve the tissue with methanol, not formalin.  Following this, special lighting is required to see the crystals.  Studies which have employed this method have shown crystals to be far more extensive than the joints.  I can't imagine that uric acid crystals in the coronary arteries initiating an inflammasome reaction is good.  Uric acid crystals don't show well on routine x-rays and scans.  It usually requires a specialized cat scan called dual energy CT (DECT scan).  These scans will often show uric acid crystals in the calcium in atherosclerotic plaques.  Unfortunately gout is treated poorly.  Give an anti-inflammatory and if it recurs we'll "think about" treatment with allopurinol or febuxostat.  Hyperuricemia, not JUST uric acid crystals, IS serious.  It's like a criminal whose fingerprints are left behind at many crime scenes- the heart, kidneys, liver, pancreas.  The tragedy is that we have good drugs and it's usually easy to treat.  Anyone diagnosed with gout or hyperuricemia should be thoroughly evaluated for heart, liver, and kidney disease.    For every molecule or uric acid produced there is also a molecule of hydrogen peroxide generated.  I've often wondered how much pathology is caused by this, as opposed to the uric acid itself.

Not all gout is caused by uric acid. Unless the joint fluid is tested, it is just assumed its the uric acid. For those on carnivore etc, if you have gout, it could be oxalate crystals. Stored up oxalates (from when plants were eaten) get released from the body in cycles (can take years). If the load is too much to excrete at once, the oxalates get 'dumped' around the body waiting for excretion, causing many different symptoms. Its common for the oxalate crystals to be     found in joints, and big toe gout. Feels the same as uric acid crystals... Most doctors assume big toe pain and swelling is uric acid crystals but there are other causes for the same symptoms. Sally Norton is the expert for oxalates. She explains how to slow down the 'dumping' and manage the symptoms.

There is a theory espoused by some that asserts insulin "resistance" is not pathological, rather that this cross-inhibition becomes extreme due to chronic overdosing of exogenous glucose. The cell eventually (being replete with substrate) ceases to uptake despite increased insulin precisely for the purpose of preventing glycation of the interior of the cell. The body, presumably, is designed to sacrifice the red blood cells and those of the interior layer of the vascular tree because they are more easily replaced over muscle or organ tissue. The upshot being that "insulin resistance" is NOT a pathology, it is a normal response to glucose toxicity.

Okay, normal healthy people, with normal BSL burn fat by beta oxidation as a default.  The acetyl co A and NADH inhibit glycolysis so glucose burn is stopped.    When glucose rises insulin rises and glut 4 let's glucose into cells, glycolysis creates malonyl CoA increase and this stops CPT1 pulling fatty acids into mitochondria, so beta oxidation stops.    Type 2 diabetics have high blood sugar, high fats and high insulin.    The high insulin prevents beta oxidation in body tissues as well as ketone formation in the liver.  So no fat utilisation possible.  Meanwhile, glut 4 fails to allow glucose into cells (insulin resistance), except the liver which then converts the glucose to fat which is stored causing  FLD.  Thus, body cells starved of fuel while excess is in blood.      The brain cells don't do beta oxidation, can't get glucose in because Glut4 doesn't work to pull in glucose (insulin resistance) and so neurones are starved of fuel while being bathed in glucose.    The hypothalamus hunger satiety centre, being IR, as a futile action,  triggers hunger to acquire more food, so making the problem worse.  Positive feedback established leading to morbidity.  Solution is to cut dietary carbohydrate and allow beta oxidation and ketone formation to fuel all cells.

以下是 《54: Saturated Fat with Dr. Ben Bikman》 的詳細繁體中文摘要：  在本集節目中，Ben Bikman 博士深入探討了飽和脂肪的科學與健康影響，並打破了大眾對飽和脂肪的長期誤解。  1. 飽和脂肪的基本認知 👌👌飽和脂肪是一類穩定的脂肪分子，在加熱與保存過程中不易氧化。  常見於動物脂肪（如牛油、豬油、奶油）以及部分植物油（如椰子油、棕櫚油）。  ❤❤在人類歷史飲食中，長期攝取飽和脂肪並未引發大規模心血管疾病的流行。  2. 與膽固醇的關係 傳統觀點：飽和脂肪會提高 LDL 膽固醇，增加心臟病風險。 Bikman 指出：飽和脂肪主要提高的是大型、低密度 LDL 顆粒，這類 LDL 並非真正的致病因素；更危險的是小而密集的 LDL，❤❤這與高碳水飲食關係更大。(TG <70mg/dL GOOD).  飽和脂肪同時可提升 HDL（「好」膽固醇），改善總膽固醇/HDL 比值。 (你說的這點其實在營養學界有一定討論空間。確實，有一些研究指出飽和脂肪的攝取不僅會提升 LDL（「壞」膽固醇），也會同時提升 HDL（「好」膽固醇），而且總膽固醇/HDL 的比值（一個比單純總膽固醇更能反映心血管風險的指標）可能因此沒有惡化，甚至在某些情況下改善。  👍👍不過，這並不代表飽和脂肪就完全無害，因為：  不同來源的飽和脂肪（例如紅肉、乳製品、椰子油）對血脂的影響差異頗大。  總體飲食型態（是否含高糖、精製碳水、足夠纖維與不飽和脂肪）會影響飽和脂肪的效果。  💕💕長期大量攝取飽和脂肪仍與部分心血管疾病風險增加有關。  👉 也就是說，飽和脂肪的「提升 HDL、改善比值」確實存在，但需要放在整體飲食與生活型態的脈絡中看待，並非單純結論「多吃沒問題」。(❤❤increase ApoB)  3. 飽和脂肪與胰島素、代謝健康 飽和脂肪對胰島素敏感性的直接負面影響有限。  在高胰島素狀態（如高糖飲食或胰島素阻抗）下，身體容易過度儲存脂肪，但這並非由飽和脂肪單獨造成。  💕💕在低碳或生酮飲食中，飽和脂肪更容易被安全轉化為能量，而非堆積。  4. 飽和脂肪在全食物中的意義 天然食物中的飽和脂肪通常伴隨其他營養素（如維生素 A、D、K2 以及共軛亞油酸）。  問題多出在工業化加工食品：當飽和脂肪與精製碳水、糖同時存在時，會導致代謝失衡。  5. 飲食建議與平衡 ❤❤不必刻意避免飽和脂肪，但應與單元不飽和脂肪及多元不飽和脂肪保持平衡。  特別需注意 Omega-6 與 Omega-3 的比例，避免過量攝取高 Omega-6 的植物油。  最佳來源：草飼牛肉、草飼奶製品、椰子油、雞蛋、野生魚類等天然食物。  6. 總結觀點 飽和脂肪不是心血管疾病或肥胖的根源，真正的關鍵在於飲食結構與代謝健康狀態。  相較於限制飽和脂肪，減少精製糖與精製碳水、維持胰島素敏感性更為重要。  💡 核心 Takeaway： 飽和脂肪並非「有毒」，而是被誤解了。在健康飲食與良好代謝環境下，❤❤😊😊適量(not over 15% calories)的飽和脂肪不僅無害，還可能對荷爾蒙、細胞膜及能量代謝有益。  我可以幫你整理 Bikman 博士在節目中引用的研究數據、實驗結論，以及用圖表呈現脂肪與膽固醇的互動機制，方便快速記憶與分享。

No, sorry, that's not correct. New cases of autoimmune diabetes are mostly diagnosed in adults, but we are stuck with this lingering belief that "Type 1 diabetes happens mostly in children."  Nope, it happens in every decade of life, and if adults were tested more, we would all see that autoimmunity is actually more prevalent than "the medical establishment" has led us to believe. What would "late" be anyway? Teens, 20s, 30s, 40s, 50s, 60s, 70s, 80s. I've talked to many individuals and seen reports of all these ages. So, there's no dividing line between "Type 1" and "Late onset Type 1."   As you say: LADA is just Type 1 diabetes. But you mentioned it in context of Type 2, and saying people are also developing autoimmunity. No, I don't see this at all. Mostly adults are simply being misdiagnosed as Type 2 from the start. And generally that is because of this old belief about Types are determined by age. Or that "gestational diabetes predicts Type 2 diabetes in 50% of cases" -- um, except sometimes it isn't Type 2, and is again autoimmune diabetes that was going to show up eventually, if the pregnancy hadn't revealed it. One can can have "double diabetes" -- with both autoimmunity and resistance. But that isn't LADA (nor is that the so-called "Type 1.5"). Type 1 are  not immune from developing insulin resistance and developing fatty deposits, for all the same reasons as you discuss.  So, would appreciate a clarification that "LADA" is not related to Type 2 in any material way; autoimmunity is the problem. And also that saying "late" is misleading and potentially harmful to patients, because they end up delaying essential insulin therapy, either because they don't want to believe they have Type 1 autoimmunity; or because their doctor doesn't want to believe it. (They might also get this nonsense diagnosis of "Type 1.5.") So, another reason medications don't work for some diabetics is because they are being treated for the wrong disease. When in fact, they cannot make enough insulin, and do absolutely need insulin injections. But these people are getting exposed to all of the wrong messages: "Insulin for Type 2 is life threatening." Okay, for too many genuine T2D patients -- but people with the incorrect T2D diagnosis walk away with an aversion to taking insulin, and may end up hospitalized with DKA; or otherwise thinking they "failed" to take care of themselves properly, and remorsefully need insulin.  All of the other medications would be masking the reason for the high glucose.   So,  what you say here is well-reasoned for Type 2; but please also emphasize that patients first need the correct diagnosis -- autoantibodies and C-peptide. If the insulin levels are not ever measurably high, then increasing possibility that the patient does not have Type 2 insulin resistance, and may be misdiagnosed. Autoantibodies are found in "Type 1" diagnosis in 90% of people with that diagnosis. Insulin therapy shouldn't be made into the boogeyman; people need proper testing to be given the proper drugs. But too many times, they don't get it.  Thanks for your work. I have so many questions on other diabetes issues.

As a Vodder trained RMT who has been working with women with lipedema for many years I have a few questions. Thank you for putting this info out, you will have helped many people.  We have known that lipedema is a connective tissue for many years now, I have seen it presented at conferences for at least the last five years, probably longer. Though when I first trained, it was simply thought to be an adipose disease. So it's fantastic to have this updated information out there.  From my clinical experience and talking with many patients at conferences, menopause is a very big trigger for lipedema symptoms.  While the firm tissue of lipedema is evident in later stages, in stage 1/early lipedema the skin can actually be very soft and can display the more apparent connective tissue comorbidities like Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome with it's very stretchy skin.  Manual lymphatic drainage should NEVER be painful, even on highly sensitive lipedema limbs  I observe pain to be a very subjective topic. Quite often the women with lipedema I've treated say they don't really have pain, yet when I question about whether it hurts when their BP is measured or their cat walks across their legs they quickly reply "oh yes". Because so many women are in pain all the time I suspect many don't even register different types of pain and simply take it for granted. Having said that, I do acknowledge the pain is unbearable in many cases  In what I've learnt, the fat cells have this extra layer of connective tissue/collagen you mention surrounding each fat cell, so they do not behave in the same way as non-lipedema fat cells. And that connective tissue is inflammatory, so each cell is surrounded by inflammatory tissue so an anti-inflammatory way of eating is essential to keep that inflammation (and therefore pain) under better control.  Having spoken with lipedema specialist surgeons, they explain that they cannot remove every single diseased fat cell so that those remaining cells continue to grow and may become symptomatic later on. I wonder whether, when fat is removed from one area, rather than it growing back in another place, the patient is simply more aware of the untreated area which may not have seemed symptomatic prior, but now that the main area of pain has been removed, another area of pain becomes predominant. A type of gate theory of pain situation.  I didn't mean to write a novel, but I'd love to start a discussion with you on some of the above observations/questions.

Hi Dr. Bikman,  Thank you for your Metabolic Classrooms. I listen to them whenever I get a chance and review them often. I have a situation I’d like you to address.  In reference to the section starting at 27:00 where you answer the question: ”Could those [exogenous] ketones be reversed in their ketogenic pathway?” The answer was no because the liver doesn’t have a process for reversing the steps from ketone to ACoA , so ketones won’t be “stored as fat” as there is no enzymatic pathway for that to happen. A sigh of relief for a lot of people, I am sure.   I’d searched a few years ago because I had a similar but slightly different concern about exogenous ketones that came about from conversations with people following low carb and ketogenic diets. The question was “If I consume ketones, is that the same as making ketones and will it help me burn fat?”   I had my doubts that exogenous ketones, while beneficial in so many ways, would actually encourage endogenous lipolysis or fat burning, considering it is the outcome of fat burning and tightly regulated by the body. I think I found support for this idea and now I have a lot of people upset with me. See Below.    In the article titled: Metabolism of ketone bodies during exercise and training: physiological basis for exogenous supplementation,  there is a paragraph, which in summary says that ‘The Metabolic actions of ketones bodies can alter fuel selection through attenuating (decreasing) glucose utilization in peripheral tissues, anti-lipolytic effects on adipose tissue, and attenuation ( a decrease) of proteolysis in skeletal muscle.    TL:DR – Extra ketones suppresses glucose use, suppresses fat breakdown, and suppresses muscle breakdown.  Great for glucose and muscle, less so for fat loss…   A separate article titled: Ketone bodies as signaling metabolites, has the claims that 1) the ketogenic diet is unusual in that it simultaneously activates “fasting” pathways despite being a high-energy state, 2) ketogenic diets suppress fatty acid synthesis enzymes, and 3) the ketogenic diet promotes mitochondrial biogenesis.   So, altogether it seems that while ketones and ketogenic diets do increase mitochondria, reduce fatty acid synthesis, and gives the benefit of fasting while being fed,  it also prevents the body from breaking down fat, while ketone levels are elevated. In other words, when ketones are high, you are not burning your own fat. Not until your body has used the ketones, will it allow the body to burn more fat to make more ketones. It’s a self-limiting process.   For that reason, I warn people that exogenous ketones have myriad benefits, but to be careful if one of their goals is weight loss, as exogenous ketones, will be hindering fat breakdown during the time their body is benefiting from outside ketone supplies.     Can you comment on this and offer your interpretation?

Before developing knee pain, I truly had great feelings given my weight was way under controlled limits and I barely felt lethargic/sluggish or any kind of mental uneasiness like panic attacks and so on, however, with hours of exercise in sharp decline, my health is failing at a faster rate than do people I come across who never either pushed themselves to their limits but mostly stuck to moderate routine exercises.   My advice to all is that stick to moderate exercises (max one hour daily). You may be 60 to 70% and not 90% healthy but you will last longer than those who pushed themselves to their limits.

Well, since 6 years ago, I've been eating even healthier than before, but am not intermittent fasting anymore. I was diagnosed with Type II diabetes, and had some extra weight.  My wife bought this book on intermittent fasting from Dr Jason Fung.  I started with the 18h-6h fasting and then switch to a 20h-4h fasting, 5 or 6 days a week (the other days was depending on the circumstances for breakfast and lunch).  I have never felt better, lost 35 lbs, and my last blood work showed I did not have Type II diabetes anymore (the 3-month average), this is without any kind of pills.   Intermittent fasting is not only good for your belly fat and Insulin resistance, but it will help get rid of the malformed proteins that sticks to your brain cells and can cause debilitating diseases like dementia or Alzheimer. This is not me saying this, it from the many research that were done and have indicated that fasting can prevent soooo many different kind of diseases... Don’t wait, start slow, stay steady and change your life forever…
